original text:
I'm trying setup TP-LINK TL-WDN3200 adapter. lsusb can see the device.
not know what from here. But what could wrong?
Warning: Symbol version dump missing; modules will have dependencies and modversions.
The kernel had the option enabled. I've disabled and rebuild kernel.
I've compiled and installed the drivers. system tells that connected the network. can see even router, but still have internet access. can't ping internal external IPs. Any suggestion?
I've tried disable security router and was working without but I'd like use security. have wpa2 personal with AES encryption wireless network.
config_ra0="192.168.1.11 netmask 255.255.0 brd 192.1.
WPA: Installing GTK the driver (keyidx=1 tx=0 len=16).
anybody can help get the security working?

original text:
there more efficient way this conditional cross-join?
Abstracting the situation: are monitoring the condition variety units, looking each unit for recorded period time. any single point time, there may nearly 8000 units currently being monitored.
Every unit has (required), start_date (required), and end_date (may null, i.e. still being monitored), and the date the most recent info collected for the unit (latest_data; equal some date later than start_date).
Every unit gets periodic check (review), which classifies into group, identified group_ID.
the unit has not been checked yet, then review_date and group_ID are both null.
What need (for the data warehouse) create record the details for each unit for every day its life the system, including its most recent review date and current group_ID.
the unit still being monitored, i.e. does not have end_date, then the daily records should continue the date the most recent info collected for the unit, sysdate data was received yet.
When table_1 and table_2 are the result set about 85k records.
The entire data set and query are stored and running Oracle 10.2.0.3.
have tried number different configurations for this query, and this one seems the most efficient, but still runs rather slowly about minutes per month data hours per (fiscal) year data.
currently perusing various Oracle docs and other resources query tuning, but it's lot wade through, and I'm not finding much that directly relates this kind situation.
Any suggestions, preferably with explanations and/or references would very appreciated.
EDIT: Please see reply and for corrections, and reply for sample data and output.
Re: there more efficient way this conditional cross-join?
This way you don't need the aggregation.
Please post create table and some sample data.

Yes, have tried that way the past, and does not appear nearly efficient. Using production data, the revised query below takes several times longer execute than first query.
You can compare the EXPLAIN PLANs for the two versions the query posted above, they are essentially identical.
difficult for get SQL Trace output files for TKPROF (because the server remote, file system remote access controls, whatnot else...), but will try. I'm still trying understand EXPLAIN PLAN and TKPROF output, anyway.


When left join table_2 why not r.rd=cal. day
you can also try connect table_1 generate rows between two dates. don't have oracle now will play with tomorrow.

Add CR.latest_data), CR.latest_data, sysdate) column the subquery "CR" and then reference below query and try use "EXISTS"

The first thing you need generate the execution plan for the query; preferably using method that generates rowsource execution stats that can see where the work goes.
Oracle may not able see how large the calendar set (perhaps assuming one row), adding the hints /*+ no_merge cardinality(1200) the select that generates the calendar may help the optimizer choose better plan.
haven't considered how this might applied your query, but it's possible that merge join targetted calendar date (cal.date start_date) may better than cross join, especially many the dates are the near past rather than the far past.
"group_ID CR.ID, CR.start_date, CR.end_date, cal.day, cal.month"
Should this "group CR.ID .." "group "group_id, cr.id ..."


Thanks, will try your suggestions either tomorrow Monday, when have time.
I'm familiar with WHERE EXISTS clauses, but can you more specific?
Thanks, I'll try using the no_merge hint.
I'm not familiar with the "partition outer join" syntax, I'll look into it.
And thanks for the reference, I'll have read that tomorrow Monday, too.
Re: Re: there more efficient way this conditional cross-join?
tried your code got 2125 rows for the first version, 2126 for the second though had delete the reference t1.month from the second get the SQL execute.
There's not really lot choose between them.
Obviously cardinality error means Oracle has chosen "hash join outer" operation rather than swapping the join inputs and using "hash join right outer", but that's detail that can fixed.
the first plan the Nested Loop join result the same cardinality error but that too can fixed give hash join.
It's hard tell whether the hash join (dates/table_1) the connect just table_1 will the more resource intensive generally would cautious about the recursive approach, but it's comparing the two.
10.
Thanks Jonathan, did change t1.month test but forgot post the correct version.
Because it's selected level one connect by. This can easily filtered out adding "start with" condition but have feeling that this row needed because used left join the first place.
The cardinality hard predict because depends latest_data each row. The whole point the rewrite replace the left join (R.rd C_cal.day R.rd null) with equal join condition, and get rid the aggregation the end. Hopefully the window for each not too big.
11.
Okay, didn't connect the comment you'd put the second statement with the one extra row that appeared.
It's going interesting see how well the strategy works particularly, perhaps, when the full row lengths are included the query. You've got rid the range-based join (which very elegant) but it's still hiding (sort of) inside the connect pump.as the data volume grows from rows 2125; and still come point where the window sort the end has sort the entire data set. Strangely the plan for code similar other OPs manages get the result without doing window sort (though doing sort group may the multiplication data that then aggregated late that the biggest performance problem).
P.S.. What's the significance introducing the sys_op_guid() call
12.
This because Oracle doesn't see any difference between the prior and current row.
13.
think Jonathan Lewis knows what sys_guid does Cost-Based Oracle Fundamentals one favorite book (although not understand all that fully). used visit his blog Oracle Scratchpad occasionally. may want point out something else!
14.
too, I'm huge fan Jonathan's and learn lot from him. translated some his articles into Chinese and people like them.

When converted into binary, how many bits are present IPv4 Address?
